summary: Practise attentive listening without rushing to advise, reassure or fill every silence.
objectives: Use open questions, reflection and summarising.
Notice spoken words, emotion, body language and silence.
Keep your own experiences in the background.

sections.title: Attention is an action
body: Listening is more than waiting for your turn to speak. It means giving enough attention to understand what this person is trying to communicate and checking that you have understood.
A patient may need time to find words. Silence does not automatically mean that the conversation has failed.

sections.heading: Active listening
sections.title: Active Listening with Louie Savva
sections.body: This existing video is included in the prototype for review. The written alternative below contains the key learning points.
alternative: Relax and stay attentive. Notice your own thoughts and feelings without allowing them to take over the conversation.
Avoid speaking too soon, too often or for too long. Listen to the words and to what may be communicated through mood, pace, body language or silence.
Reflect or summarise what you have heard: “It sounds as though the uncertainty has been particularly difficult.” Check rather than assume: “Have I understood that correctly?”
Avoid loaded remarks, judgement and quick reassurance. The purpose is to understand the patient’s experience, not to replace it with your own.

items.title: Open the space
items.body: “How have things been for you?” or “What has been on your mind today?”

items.title: Reflect emotion
items.body: “You sound disappointed,” offered tentatively rather than as a diagnosis.

items.title: Clarify
items.body: “When you say you have had enough, can you tell me a little more about what you mean?”

items.title: Summarise
items.body: “You have had several pieces of difficult news and it feels as though nobody has had time to explain what happens next.”

sections.label: Patient story
sections.title: Jennifer asks about you
sections.prompt: Jennifer is pleased to see you again. She asks about your family and weekend, and you discover several interests in common. How should you respond?

options.text: Share your full family history so that the relationship feels equal.
options.feedback: Some ordinary warmth is fine, but extensive self-disclosure can shift the focus and create a relationship that feels more like friendship.

options.text: Answer briefly and naturally, then bring the attention back to Jennifer and what she wants to talk about.
options.feedback: This avoids being cold or evasive while keeping the patient’s experience at the centre.

options.text: Refuse to answer any question about yourself because volunteers must never reveal anything personal.
options.feedback: Rigid refusal may feel unnatural. The skill is proportionate self-disclosure: brief, safe and in service of the patient rather than your own needs.

sections.debrief: Before sharing something personal, ask yourself: who is this for, and will it help the patient or divert the conversation?

sections.label: Be human
sections.title: You may be affected too
body: Patients may remind you of your own illness, loss or relationships. Being moved does not make you a poor volunteer. Notice the reaction, keep the patient’s experience in the foreground and speak to your mentor afterwards if you need support.
